Sentence: You step down off the bus with your field notebook tucked under one arm and the sea-glass pendant warm against your throat.
Salt tangs the air—bright and a little sharp—and the harbor smells of diesel and
wet kelp, a familiar perfume that steadies you more than it should.
The pier planks creak underfoot, patched and oiled in places, raw in others; the town's handiwork stitched into the bones of the place.
A paint-splattered skiff rocks against a repaired piling.
Gulls wheel in lazy circles above it, and the surf's hiss seems to keep time with your breathing.
Somewhere across the water, a metal panel clanks—a neighbor tightening something that will not be made by architects but by hands that remember.
Look at you, you packed half the estuary in that notebook, huh?
Only the useful half.
Your fingers tighten on the leather cover.
And the other half is probably tide-rusted.
Good.
We need somebody to tell us what rust will do next.' (He looks at the harbor) 'How was the lab up there?
Still full of Dr.
Park’s Post-its?
You let a laugh out—soft, relieved.
There's warmth in being noticed for your particular kind of compulsive cataloguing.
• Step forward and hug Rafi
You fold into him; the oilskin is rough but steady.
He pats your back like he's checking that you're the same person who left and came back.
• Keep a respectful distance and clap his shoulder
You clap his shoulder instead, the contact brisk.
He snorts as if you were being ceremonial—then nods, satisfied either way.
Good to have you back.
Elias 'Eli' Rowan's been fiddling with a salvaged inverter since last week—says it'll keep the seedlings warm when the cloud cover goes long.
Elias 'Eli' Rowan looks up at the name, goggles pushed onto his head, hair thrown into wind-stiffed spikes.
His grin is a bright, easy thing—an architecture of optimism.
Elias 'Eli' Rowan And, well, you can tell me if my scavenged inverter is heresy.' (He waves the braided cord of his remote as if it were a flag.) 'Or if it's a miracle.
I'll tell you the truth.
(fingers tightening on the leather cover) 'If it's a miracle, we'll measure it.
If it's heresy, we'll fix it together.'
Elias 'Eli' Rowan (mock solemn) Unicorns and manuals.
Sounds like a plan.
someone who wants to get it right this time, with other people, here.
You walk with them along the boardwalk; the town's patchwork becomes a conversation.
New concrete plates sit next to splintered boards, and the air holds that wet wood smell, mossy and honest.
You remember the old arcade?
Used to be right there.' (He points at a waterline stain) 'We used to eat dulse chips in summer.
I do.' Your voice is small against the sound of the sea.
'You could still find a place to hang a hammock between two memories.
Elias 'Eli' Rowan And maybe put a rain-catcher on the roof of the arcade,' he says.
'Not to replace anything, just—support it.
You stop at a planter where seedlings reach in bright, stubborn green.
Recycled solar panels catch what light there is and throw it down in thin rectangles on the boards.
The Arbor breathes—wood, glass, and the faint mineral scent of fertilizer.
solar tubing, braided gutters, and seed trays in various states of hope.
You set the notebook down on a workbench crowded with spanners and seed trays, the leather making a soft sound against rough wood.
We saved space for you.' (He reaches over and nudges a tray at the edge.) 'Figured you'd like to count leaves for breakfast.
Like clockwork.' Your fingers trace the spine of the notebook.
The ritual calms you—calibration as a prayer.
'How did the winter hold up?
Elias 'Eli' Rowan Less dramatic than last year.' He shrugs, but there's an edge in it.
'Still, tides were...funny.
Missed the usual rhythm in January.
A few houses had more puddles than their basements deserve.
That's not nothing.
Elias 'Eli' Rowan No.' He watches you study a tray of seedlings.
'You look...settled, Mara.
How long's this 'back to stay' thing going to last?
As long as I'm useful.
Elias 'Eli' Rowan That's a selfish answer.' He grins.
'Good selfish.
You give a little laugh.
It warms you, small and necessary.
• Offer Elias 'Eli' Rowan a hot thermos from your pack
You pull the thermos out—tea still steaming.
He takes it with both hands, grateful, and the warmth moves through him in soft, visible relief.
• Keep the thermos to yourself and focus on the seedlings
You tuck the thermos away.
Elias 'Eli' Rowan watches you for a beat, then nudges a packet of seed tape across the bench like a truce.
'For later,' he says.
Enough romance,' he teases.
'Get the wristband calibrated; Dr.
Park'll want the first tide-run before noon.
On it.
The lab smells like antiseptic cleaner turned a little briny at the edges.
Your fingers—callused from fieldwork, ink-streaked—unfurl the wristband and click it against your wrist.
It hums, small and obedient, solar cells blinking like patient
eyes.
The display warms into life; numbers and graphs settle into their grids, familiar as tide marks.
You set sensors along the window sill—temperature probes, salinity testers, a strand of reed tied to a marker to watch the micro-fluctuations.
The laptop fan breathes; you open the tidal model you've adapted for this estuary and feed it the seasonal parameters.
Your breath finds the rhythm of the computation.
a splintered bench,
the arcade's neon bent under salt, Grandma Hira's shawl drying after a high tide that stole her stoop.
Both perspectives weigh in; both give you tools.
One is exacting.
The other is an ache you can't
quantify.
You toggle a control and run the model.
The results appear, neat and clinical.
They are worrying in small increments—return periods for what used to be fifty-year floods shortening, flood lines inching closer to familiar streets.
Not catastrophic.
Not yet.
But the change that
used to be a rumor carved into local memory is now numbers, and numbers make new arrangements inevitable.
You feel your chest tighten—an old, familiar pressure—then relax into it.
It's not panic.
It's the sober thrill of an engineer seeing a problem clearly enough to begin fixing it.
You breathe out.
'Small shifts,' you murmur.
'Manageable.'
Elias 'Eli' Rowan looks over your shoulder, brows knitting at the projection.
'Manageable?' His voice hunts for certainty.
'Manageable how?'
(you turn the screen toward him) 'We can design buffers—green berms, distributed rain capture, modular breakwater tests.
Nothing glamorous, but maintainable by people here.
We start small, prove it works.'
Elias 'Eli' Rowan Proof is what I like to build.
We can make a prototype—here, by the Arbor, using the old dock timbers and that salvaged mesh.
Exactly.
Keep people in place, keep livelihoods intact.
We keep the town's bones.
And recipes for fixing them.
You can't have a miracle that nobody knows how to cook.
from the
hush of arrival, through the quiet exchanges, to the soft click of the model giving you a map.
You reach into your pocket and touch the sea-glass pendant at your throat.
It is cool, worn to a pale edge by years of being held.
It anchors you in a way no dataset could; it
remembers the child who played on ruined planks and the adult who will now fight for them.
Elias 'Eli' Rowan So…you're really back, huh?
For good?
You pause.
It is a question shaped like an invitation and a promise both.
Saying yes feels like deciding a tide-line—not an arrogant claim that you'll control everything, but an agreement to stay with what your home needs.
I'm home to help.
Elias 'Eli' Rowan Good.
We'll make something that works.
Together.
Rafi whistles softly—a sound like approval—and returns to his coffee, eyes twinkling.
Inside you, the scientist catalogues next steps; the community-minded person composes a plan; the private memory files away small comforts for storms to come.
something to build, someone to build with.
You pull up the model one more time to run a sensitivity analysis—tweaking parameters, imagining small interventions.
Each iteration shifts the flood line in inches and feet, sometimes better, sometimes worse.
You note them down, your handwriting neat despite the quickening pulse in your fingertips.
the numbers demand action, and the people beside you are already reaching for the tools.
You close the laptop and inhale the greenhouse air—wet, green, alive.
There is work to do.
You feel the page of the morning turn beneath your thumb.
You are at the long, scarred workbench in the Arbor.
Seedlings crowd every possible surface—small green fists of life in mismatched pots and upcycled gutters.
Your notebook lies open, seaweed pressed between pages like a slow
soil and wet burlap, a faint sweetness of compost under the sharper tang of salt.
Your wristband vibrates against your skin.
You flip up the tablet cradle and Dr.
Naomi Park’s face fills the screen—sharp glasses, grey-streaked bun wound tight; even on video she looks like someone who keeps time with tides and spreadsheets.
Mara.
Sorry to interrupt your morning.
Are you at the lab or the Arbor?
Arbor.
Good.
I need an urgent run on the estuary salinity models.
The latest sensor cluster reported a pulse overnight—sharp spike across the south channel.
If we don't map it against the tidal prism now, we lose a window to predict the intrusion.
nodes, boundary conditions, parameter uncertainties.
The scientist in you sorts the problem into manageable pieces, impatient to begin.
The community-minder in you feels the clock differently—people need pumps, nets, patchwork levees, and someone to show up with tools.
How sharp are we talking?
Enough that the eelgrass beds on the south flats could be stressed if the intrusion persists.
Naomi's voice softens—'I hate asking this, Mara, but can you prioritize a sensitivity iteration?
I can push processing from here if you initialize the run.
She waits—no flourish, just the professional quiet that means the data is already moving through her head.
I can start it.
Give me forty-five minutes for the initial grid, then I’ll hand it to you.
Forty-five.
Thank you.
And Mara—watch the southern transect when you do your field checks.
Your wristband logs might show micro-anomalies I can't see from the fixed stations.
You close the tablet and set to work, but the bench is alive with voices and tools.
Tess O'Malley appears in the doorway like a sudden gust—short red hair plastered to her collar from a misty
eager worry.
Tess O'Malley Mara—sorry to drop in.
Busy?
Always.
What's up, Tess?
Tess O'Malley A council brief—leaked, if you want to be dramatic.
Livia's proposal is on a fast track.
There are private stakeholder meetings this week, some before the public notice goes up.
I—' (she hesitates, hands folding like she's trying to keep something inside) '—I thought you should know.
You feel the space between air and metal tighten.
Fast tracks mean fixed decisions, and this town's history is full of fast tracks that left people out in the wet.
How fast?
Tess O'Malley They've scheduled a sponsor pitch for Thursday, and there's talk of bringing in outside contractors to fast-deploy sections of the barrier.
It's…moving quicker than anyone expected.
I don't know if the council will rubber-stamp it, but Livia's got momentum.
Tess's eyes search yours for a reaction.
On the bench, Elias 'Eli' Rowan is soldering something that smells faintly of ozone; Rafi's coffee steams nearby.
Your hands hover over your notebook, between pressed seaweed and a tide-chart page gone soft at the edges.
Elias 'Eli' Rowan Speaking of moving fast—want to see something?
I finished a compact harvester prototype.
Light enough for one person to position, heavy enough to hold under a spring tide.
Elias 'Eli' Rowan It could feed small fish farms and power rooftop pumps.
If we spool a few of these along the channel, they might keep the micro-lifts running even during high water.
biofouling, drag coefficients, maintenance load.
It's promising.
What's the torque on that rotor at three-knot flow?
Elias 'Eli' Rowan About—' (he says a number, then checks a scribble on his notepad) '—enough to turn a micro-generator.
I've got a mounting idea that won't need cranes.
We could prototype a line in a weekend.
He looks at you as if the plan is already a shared map.
His gray-blue eyes are open and steady.
Elias 'Eli' Rowan We can test it today if you want.
A small warmth rises in your palm where his glance lands; his elbow brushes yours.
His hand finds yours briefly—not a gesture saturated with history, but steadying.
The contact is soft and quick, a human cable across a table of tools.
Later.
I need Naomi's run started and a field reading before we deploy anything upstream.
The models will tell us where to place tests so we don't stress the beds.
Elias 'Eli' Rowan Okay.
Models first.
Then we build.
That's a good order.
• Start Naomi's model now, alone
You close your notebook, set up the initial grid, and let the early iterations unwrap.
The familiar rhythm of numbers calms you; you like seeing patterns take shape.
• Ask Eli to help set up the model, then head to the tide line together
You gesture for Eli to bring his spare laptop.
He props the harvester beside the tablet and, with a hack of humour, inputs boundary conditions.
The two of you fit into a quick, efficient rhythm—hands and minds working together.
you pull the laptop into the sun and start the grid while Eli boots a secondary device to carry to the field.
Rafi offers a wry whistle.
That's the only way this place runs—people doing two things at once.
metal clinks, muted laughter, and the intermittent beep of your wristband as it samples the air.
Thirty minutes feels like both an instant and a lifetime of small decisions.
You walk the tide line with Elias 'Eli' Rowan and Rafi.
The sky is a pale, low bowl that keeps the sound close.
The tide is out enough to reveal mud and the pale, trembling of
roots.
Your wristband chirps when you dip it into the shallows; a small readout blooms on your wrist—pH, conductivity, micro-sediment turbidity.
You scribble numbers with a pen that smells of salt, the page shaking in your
hand.
The south channel is the town's pulse.
If salt pushes in, the pulse changes.
We won't notice immediately, but ecosystems will.
The wristband ticks again.
A small anomaly blinks in the conductivity stream—slightly elevated in narrow pockets near a collapsed jetty.
You circle it on the map and mark a transect.
You kids remember the night the old boardwalk came apart?
I was here then.
Looked like a curtain call—boards popped up and the whole thing folded like theater scenery.
He squints toward a ragged half-plank still clinging to rusted bolts.
Felt wrong that night.
Sounded wrong.
People talk about losing things twice—first the wood, then the memory.
We don't want that here again.
it's a town memory that warms and stings.
It makes your hands tighten on the clipboard.
Elias 'Eli' Rowan If we place a string of harvesters along that stretch, we could reduce flow energy in those pockets and give the beds time to recover.
They could act like living breakwaters—modular, replaceable, community-maintained.
You and Elias 'Eli' Rowan discuss placement—lengths, moorings, how to avoid scouring eelgrass.
Your voice is low; you both know the models will refine his instincts if you run them properly.
But there's momentum in his
plan that could be used now, in a weekend, to show people a tangible option.
a policy decision could rearrange everything.
• Encourage Eli to set up a quick pilot at the pocket now
You nod to Eli.
He grins, quick and relieved, and together you haul the module into the shallows.
It squeaks and bobbles, then settles, bearing its first pull.
The community around you notices; a small crowd forms.
• Insist on finishing the model run before any deployment
model, then build.

You step into the Council Hall carrying the leftover warmth of the rooftop garden on your skin—damp palms, the faint iron of compost in your nose—and the knot of urgency from the morning clinches again.
The
room smells of brass polish and stale coffee; those scents settle over you like an official coat.
Livia Chen sits at the dais with her exacting posture and that black bob that always reads like authority.
Her trench coat is immaculate; the brooch on her lapel—an angular wave—glints.
She unfolds glossy renderings across
a sweeping curved seawall, a mixed-use promenade, investor logos arranged like constellations.
The slides move with the mechanical certainty of a well-rehearsed argument.
This design is immediate, comprehensive, and funded.
Jobs, infrastructure, safety.
We don't have the luxury of small experiments while the tide rises.
Her voice is practiced—warm at the edges, clinical inside.
You taste the artifice like metal on your tongue.
Around you, industry representatives nod; a man in a dark jacket taps the edge of his brochure like a metronome.
Tess O'Malley Funding milestones.
Covenants.
It's not unconditional—check the fine print on phase two.
You don't read it yet.
You tuck the paper away.
For a second you picture the rooftop garden—Grandma Hira's hands, Eli's grin—and the urge to keep those hands intact is a physical tug at your ribs.
• Breathe slowly and center
You inhale, counting to four.
Your pulse lengthens its rhythm; you let the room condense into a single, reachable point.
• Check your wristband for the latest sensor readout
The display blinks tidal graphs and a steady green; the data steadies you in its blunt honesty.
• Look at Eli across the room
Eli's fingers are threaded through his toolkit strap; his eyes meet yours and then look away—the moment holds, complicated and thin.
The scene continues.
You take the podium after Livia finishes, the microphone catching your breath and making it too loud.
Your notes are there—neat handwriting, tide charts and schematic sketches pressed flat by a thumb smudged with potting soil.
You can feel the room holding its breath with you.
The fluorescent light casts a halo over your page, translating everything into hard edges.
Our simulations don't promise a total fix.
They reduce wave energy at the neighborhoods most at risk.
They keep livelihoods—fishermen, small shops—intact.
They can be built with local labor, repaired by the same hands that will rely on them.
A murmur ripples.
Grandma Hira's hand clasps the edge of her shawl; her face is taut with a plea you don't need to parse.
Rafi stands at the back like an old mast—steady, solid—nodding once.
Industry Rep With respect, those are pilot projects.
Pilots in the face of escalating storms are compromises you can't afford.
You hear the accusation in his tone—an implied moral failing.
You counter with statistics, with sensitivity analyses that show how distributed measures change flood line projections by measurable margins in plausible scenarios.
You lay out the maintenance plans, the training programs, the phased cost curves.
But the chamber remembers other rules—speed, scale, the siren call of capital.
Livia rises, the slide behind her switching to a glossy rendering of a thriving promenade and an numbers board that reads like an investor pitch.
Scale saves people quickly.
The shore needs a decisive, unified barrier.
Fragmented solutions cost more in the long run; they delay action.
a brief hard set at the corner of her
covenant strings.
You can't tell if that string will hold the promise or pull the whole
town apart.
Elias 'Eli' Rowan rises after you.
He walks to the microphone with the casual certainty of someone who has built things from scrap and made them sing.
His voice is bright—open, but with an edge that carries the weight of scars.
Elias 'Eli' Rowan This isn't either-or.
You can put a seawall where it makes sense and—' (he glances at Livia) '—but you also have to keep the neighborhoods functioning, the crews employed, the knowledge passed on.
If we outsource everything to a wall, where do the people go when the wall fails?
There is a shift in the room.
A few heads tilt; some councilors exchange glances.
Eli's words land like a small hammer on a brittle surface and it cracks enough for sound to move through.
Mr.
Rowan, what you propose sounds—idealistic.
The council has to think in population protection.
Logistics.
One coordinated barrier is administratively simpler.
Elias 'Eli' Rowan Simpler for whom?' (he looks at the audience, then at you) 'For the contractors, perhaps.
For the people who live here, simpler can mean abandonment.
The exchange grows teeth.
A woman with a campaign lapel — a backer of Livia's plan — coughs loudly; someone shouts for 'jobs.' Grandma Hira stands now, and when she speaks her voice is small but sharpened by history.
I have seen the boardwalk swallowed once.
We sang while we mended it.
We buried the ones the water took.
You tell me to move every time.
When do we keep what we love?
Her question lands like a bell.
The room tightens.
The air tastes faintly of salt and fear.
You find yourself parsing motives like sea charts.
Livia genuinely believes in order through scale; Tess's notes suggest legal levers that can bind the promise; Rafi's posture says he will show up to build; Eli's earnestness
is tempered by past mistakes he barely mentions.
You feel, underneath everything, the fragile thing that lives between you and Eli—trust that might hold or fray depending on the choice you make.
Council Chair Ms.
Kestrel, the council looks to you for a recommendation.
Your models and experience are central.
The town needs a path forward.
Speak plainly.
the brass reflecting the light like cold scales, the ocean beyond the windows a flat, indifferent witness.
A flurry of rapid exchanges erupts as councilors seek clarification—line items, maintenance responsibilities, insurance clauses.
Voices overlap; the projector hums louder.
The tempo rises; your pulse keeps perfect tempo with it.
You count the breaths without meaning to.
Each second stretches and compresses.
• Lean into the data — make a clinical, hard argument
precise, unromantic.
The room listens for certainty; some appreciate it, others recoil at the dryness.
• Tell Grandma Hira's story first — anchor the policy in people
You let emotion lace your words.
Faces soften; some councilors shift uncomfortably, unsure whether to be moved or pragmatic.
The scene continues.
Elias 'Eli' Rowan steps forward, placing a hand briefly near your elbow—not touching, but close enough that the heat of his presence reads as an offering and a question.
You look at him.
There is so
trust with a partner, trust with a town, trust with a history that refuses to be measured only in spreadsheets.
Livia watches you with that calibrated gaze.
For the first time her smile is not quite gentle; it is an assessment.
Her next words are lower, a steel wire beneath velvet.
Ms.
Kestrel, your models are compelling in controlled conditions.
The reality is messier.
The council needs a plan that politics and markets will fund without years of piloting.
We can begin construction this season.
Delay risks lives.
You bite a thought back.
The word 'delay' becomes a blade that slices through every softer alternative.
You feel the room pivot between fear of inaction and fear of wrong action.
The air is charged—electric, too tight to hold without snapping.
We've weathered storms together.
We can learn to fix our own seams.
But we need the tools and the rights to work.
Don't hand us a promise that locks us out.
An industry rep scoffs; a young councilor whispers about ballots and polls.
The noise is a swarm.
Your throat is dry.
The projector blinks.
Somewhere beyond the windows the sea presses the horizon like an accusation.
Council Chair This chamber needs a resolution.
The vote will follow your recommendation, Ms.
Kestrel.
How do you advise we proceed?
You inhale.
The whole room holds its breath with you.
Your heart hammers like a signal drum in a gale.
Everything you have worked for—your notebooks, the rooftop, the people who taught you how to read
tide lines—presses close.
So does the person on the benches whose hands you have come to find steady in the dark.
a street-level movement bound in the dirt and calluses of neighbors; a sweeping contract
that could secure funding but estrange the people who trusted you; or a brittle hybrid that makes you the hinge, admired and targeted in equal measure.
The chamber tightens to a single point.
The projector fades to a blank white while the council waits on your next words.
You are aware, with the precise clarity of someone standing on a cliff, that whatever you choose now will not simply be a policy.
It will be a promise—or a wound—to the town, to Eli, to yourself.
You swallow and prepare to speak.
You are standing where the chamber feels narrowest—shoulder-to-shoulder with Livia Chen, her trench coat immaculate at the collar, the fabric swallowing the studio lights.
Behind her, investors lean forward like a pack sensing weather.
In front
of you, the council benches bloom into a small orchard of polished suits and blinking devices.
The sea beyond the glass presses against the horizon, dark and indifferent.
funding lines stamped, supply contracts nodding into being.
The ledger world is opening up—concrete, measurable, immediate.
But your chest is a locked drum.
The rhythm of it is too loud.
You can feel your field notebook thick at your hip like a guilty thing.
Measured scientific leadership—exactly what the town needed, Mara.
You cut through theater.' (She slides a tablet across the lectern—slick glass, a wallpaper of deadline bars and red markers.
The funding windows blink like small alarms.) 'This window doesn't wait.
Three bids in ninety days.
Milestones every quarter.
Oversight committee to monitor compliance.
procurement, environmental assessments compressed into bureaucratic time, contractors pre-cleared.
The fonts are clean; the risks are packaged
in bullet points.
Your stomach drops into the tone of someone who has measured too many probabilities and found one that will cost more than numbers can show.
Tess O'Malley, standing a few steps back with her fluorescent lanyard clutched like a talisman, leans in.
Her eyes are wide, raw.
Tess O'Malley Ms.
Kestrel—will there be guarantees for the neighborhoods outside the plan?
The—' (she swallows) '—the ones with the older foundations?
Livia Chen gives the practiced smile that has saved more meetings than you can count.
Livia Chen We will mitigate.
There are buyout options.
Transition assistance.
Jobs.
We will bring engineering to the table.' (Her voice smooths to assurance.) 'And with Mara’s recommendations integrated, this will be science-led and accountable.
Tess O'Malley’s shoulders twitch; the lanyard digs into her palm.
Tess O'Malley That's not what people outside say.
They're afraid—
Fear is understandable.
But fear is not a plan.' (She looks at you, and the look is both a benediction and a billing statement.) 'We needed an expert to make the case, Mara.
You did it.
You gave them something they could fund.
You nod because your lungs have no better move.
You can feel Elias 'Eli' Rowan watching you from the gallery—his grey-blue eyes narrowed, jaw set like a hinge.
He looks like someone who has just walked
into a dance where he doesn't know the steps and his palms smell of solvent.
His paint-splattered jumpsuit reads like a defiant map of every late night he’s spent trying to fix things without burning them.
He stands an island of grit amid the varnished crowd.
sharp, charged, full of static.
Voice from the back You sold us out.
You taste metal.
Your hands are not steady.
Livia Chen squeezes your arm in a way that should be comforting but feels like a contract signature.
Livia Chen They'll see the jobs, the stabilization—give it time, Mara.
The optics shift the moment the first pier goes up.
You try to answer and nothing you say will rearrange the room.
Your training reaches for risk matrices and sensitivity analyses; it finds only the jagged edge of human consequence.
The spreadsheet in your head begins to noisily enumerate who benefits and who is bumped—so many small, irreplaceable line items.
• Reassure Tess quietly
'We will insist on covenants—community seats on oversight.
I'll push for them.' She nods once, fragile, and clings to the hope like a rope.
• Turn to Elias 'Eli' Rowan and meet his gaze
You catch Elias 'Eli' Rowan's eye and let a laugh that isn't funny escape you.
He widens his brow, as if you have handed him a map with the wrong coordinates.
His jaw hardens; he says nothing, but his silence tells you the shape of things to come.
contracts, NDAs, legal pads scrawled with clauses.
You leave the hall under a soft rain of flash interviews and TV crews.
Livia Chen walks a step ahead, radiating the kind of calm that is either certainty or practiced performance.
Her brooch reflects back a jagged sun streak; the image of a wave is pretty and strategically placed.
Her voice is lower now, only for you.
Livia Chen We need your signatures on the oversight schedule tonight.
We need the lab to begin baseline sampling tomorrow.
Will you brief the engineers, Mara?
The ask is precise.
The hour is small.
Your reply tastes like a resignation letter.
You say yes.
tidy stacks of proposals, risk matrices, CAD prints.
The materials are cleanly laid out like soldiers in formation.
Lead Engineer (Kai) Ms.
Kestrel, we ran the hydrodynamic model against the proposed wall configuration.
Initial projections reduce overtopping by seventy percent at design storm thresholds.
Construction phase—eighteen months.
Maintenance schedule—quarterly.
We've identified anchor points, prefabrication sites, and supply chains.
a clinical litany of success metrics.
Each statistic is a promise written in steel.
But in the spaces between them your mind keeps drawing maps of households and shrines that will be moved or submerged—places for which there are no budget lines.
You ask And displacement?
Secondary flooding?
Where do the modeled impacts shift the risk?
Kai taps the screen, fingers precise.
Kai We optimized for the town's protected core.
Peripheral zones show increased shear stress—some redistribution.
We've flagged those for mitigation study.
We can pilot armoring, tide-evac routes, or selective elevation.
Your breath catches.
The engineers' language is a comfort in its clarity, and a knife in its inevitability.
Every optimization has a byproduct; every displacement has coordinates.
A door opens and Dr.
Naomi Park steps in, hair knotting the fluorescent air like a banner.
Her presence tightens the room.
Dr.
Naomi Park Mara.' (She folds her arms, gaze hard.) 'I read the brief.
Centralized infrastructure is clean on paper—but remember history.
We concentrate protection, we push vulnerability to neighboring communities.
